overview.introduction: The Linux command line is the foundation of DevOps. Every cloud server, container, and CI/CD pipeline runs on Linux — and is managed through a terminal, not a GUI.
overview.why_it_matters: Over 90% of cloud servers run Linux. When a production server crashes at 2am, you SSH in and fix it from the terminal — no mouse, no GUI, just commands.
overview.industry_usage: Used daily by DevOps engineers, SREs, backend developers, and cloud architects to manage servers, debug pipelines, write automation scripts, and configure infrastructure.
overview.what_you_will_build: A set of shell commands to create a project directory structure, write configuration files, and navigate the Linux file system — all from the terminal.
overview.prerequisites: None — zero prior terminal experience required.
learning_outcomes: Navigate the Linux file system using pwd, cd, and ls.
Create, read, copy, move, and delete files and directories.
Write text into files using echo and redirection operators.
Understand the difference between absolute and relative paths.
Identify your user context and terminal environment.

key_concepts.title: whoami (Identity)
key_concepts.definition: A command that tells you the username of the account you are currently logged into.
key_concepts.explanation: In a text-only interface, whoami is like checking your profile badge to see who you are.

commands.name: whoami
commands.purpose: Shows the username of your current session.
commands.output_explanation: This shows you are logged in as the user named 'student'.

key_concepts.title: pwd (Location)
key_concepts.definition: Stands for 'Print Working Directory'. It tells you the exact folder path you are currently looking at.
key_concepts.explanation: Since there are no visual folders in a terminal, pwd acts as your GPS to show your current location.

commands.name: pwd
commands.output_explanation: This means you are inside the 'student' folder, which is inside 'home'.

key_concepts.title: ls, ls -a, & ls -l (Listing Files)
key_concepts.definition: Commands to list the files and folders inside your current location.
key_concepts.explanation: Use ls to see visible files. Add -a to see hidden files (starting with a dot). Add -l to see detailed information like file sizes and permissions.

commands.name: ls
commands.output_explanation: Shows the folder drafts and file welcome.txt in your current folder.

commands.name: ls -a
commands.purpose: Lists all files including hidden ones (starting with a dot).
commands.output_explanation: Shows hidden files like .bashrc along with visible files.

commands.name: ls -l
commands.purpose: Lists files with details like permissions, size, and owner.
commands.output_explanation: Shows permissions (-rw-r--r--), owner (student), size (45 bytes), and name.

key_concepts.title: cd & cd ~ (Moving Around)
key_concepts.definition: Stands for 'Change Directory'. It lets you move from one folder to another.
key_concepts.explanation: cd followed by a folder name moves you into that folder. Typing cd with a tilde (cd ~) or cd alone takes you straight back to your home folder.

commands.name: cd
commands.output_explanation: Moves your active terminal view into the drafts folder.

commands.name: cd ~
commands.purpose: Instantly takes you back to your home folder.
commands.output_explanation: Returns you directly to /home/student from anywhere.

key_concepts.title: touch & mkdir (Creating Files & Folders)
key_concepts.definition: touch creates a new empty file. mkdir creates a new empty folder.
key_concepts.explanation: These commands let you start new files or organize your work by setting up folder structures.

commands.name: touch
commands.output_explanation: Creates an empty file named draft.txt.

commands.name: mkdir
commands.purpose: Creates a new empty directory folder.
commands.output_explanation: Creates a new folder named projects.

key_concepts.title: echo, >, & >> (Writing Content)
key_concepts.definition: echo prints text to the screen. > and >> redirect that text to write it into a file.
key_concepts.explanation: Using > writes text to a file and erases whatever was in it before. Using >> appends the text to the end of the file without erasing anything.

commands.name: echo with >
commands.purpose: Writes text to a file, overwriting existing content.
commands.output_explanation: Writes 'Hello' into draft.txt, replacing any old content.

commands.name: echo with >>
commands.purpose: Appends text to the end of a file.
commands.output_explanation: Adds 'World' to the end of draft.txt on a new line.

key_concepts.title: cat (Viewing Files)
key_concepts.definition: Displays all the text content inside a file on your terminal screen.
key_concepts.explanation: It is a quick way to read the contents of a text file or configuration file without opening a text editor.

commands.name: cat
commands.output_explanation: Prints the content of draft.txt directly to the screen.

key_concepts.title: cp & mv (Copy & Move)
key_concepts.definition: cp copies a file to a new location. mv moves or renames a file.
key_concepts.explanation: cp leaves the original file where it was. mv shifts the original file to a new place or renames it in place.

commands.name: cp
commands.purpose: Copies a file to a new path.
commands.output_explanation: Creates a duplicate copy of draft.txt named draft_backup.txt.

commands.name: mv
commands.output_explanation: Moves draft.txt inside the projects folder.

key_concepts.title: rm (Deleting Files)
key_concepts.definition: Stands for 'remove'. It permanently deletes files.
key_concepts.explanation: Warning: rm deletes files immediately and permanently. There is no recycle bin or trash folder in the terminal.

interactive_examples.objective: Find out who you are logged in as.
interactive_examples.scenario: You SSH into a remote server and need to confirm your identity before running admin commands.
interactive_examples.explanation: The shell queries the OS for the username associated with your active session.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: You are currently running commands as the 'student' user — not root.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: DevOps scripts run whoami to verify they have the correct permissions before applying system configurations.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Typing 'who am i' (with spaces) — this exists but gives more verbose output about all logged-in users.
interactive_examples.tips: Use `id` for even more detail: user ID, group ID, and all group memberships.

interactive_examples.objective: Find your current location in the file system.
interactive_examples.scenario: Before editing or deleting files, always confirm where you are to avoid mistakes.
interactive_examples.explanation: pwd (Print Working Directory) outputs the absolute path from the root / to your current directory.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: You are inside the student user's home directory.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Run pwd at the start of every script to log the working directory for debugging.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Assuming you know where you are after using cd — always confirm with pwd.
interactive_examples.tips: On most systems, the prompt shows ~ when you are in your home directory, so pwd confirms the actual path.

interactive_examples.objective: List all files including hidden configuration files.
interactive_examples.scenario: You are debugging why a user's shell behaves differently — you need to check their .bashrc and .profile files.
interactive_examples.explanation: The -l flag shows long format (permissions, owner, size, date) and -a shows all files including those starting with a dot (hidden files).
interactive_examples.expected_output:
total 28
drwxr-xr-x 3 student student 4096 Jan 12 .
drwxr-xr-x 5 root    root    4096 Jan 12 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 student student  220 Jan 12 .bash_logout
-rw-r--r-- 1 student student 3526 Jan 12 .bashrc
drwxr-xr-x 2 student student 4096 Jan 12 drafts
-rw-r--r-- 1 student student   45 Jan 12 welcome.txt
interactive_examples.output_explanation: Hidden files (.bash_logout, .bashrc) are now visible. The drafts directory and welcome.txt are regular visible items.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Check ~/.ssh/ directory to verify SSH authorized_keys file exists with correct permissions.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Using ls -A instead of ls -a — ls -A hides . and .. entries but shows other hidden files.
interactive_examples.tips: Use `ls -lah` to also show file sizes in human-readable format (KB, MB) instead of raw bytes.

interactive_examples.objective: Create a config file and write content into it.
interactive_examples.scenario: You are setting up a new application and need to create an environment variables file.
interactive_examples.explanation: touch creates the file. echo writes the text. cat verifies the content was written correctly. The && chains commands — each runs only if the previous succeeded.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: The .env file was created, PORT=3000 was written into it, and cat confirms the content.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Create environment configuration files during application deployment automation.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Using > when you meant >> — this overwrites the entire file instead of appending.
interactive_examples.tips: Use >> to append additional settings: echo 'DB_HOST=localhost' >> .env

interactive_examples.objective: Back up a file before editing it.
interactive_examples.scenario: You need to modify a system configuration but want a safety net in case something breaks.
interactive_examples.explanation: cp creates an exact duplicate. ls -la /etc/hosts* then lists all files starting with 'hosts' to confirm both exist.
interactive_examples.expected_output:
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 221 Jan 12 /etc/hosts
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 221 Jan 12 /etc/hosts.bak
interactive_examples.output_explanation: Both files exist with identical sizes, confirming the backup was successful.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Always backup nginx.conf, sshd_config, or fstab before editing system configuration files.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Forgetting to backup before editing — if you break the config, you lose the original.
interactive_examples.tips: Use a timestamp in the backup name: cp file.conf file.conf.$(date +%Y%m%d) for version tracking.

lessons.title: 1. Terminal Introduction (whoami)
lessons.definition: The terminal is a text-based interface used to control a computer.
lessons.explanation: Instead of clicking icons, you type commands. The system executes them and displays results.
lessons.instruction: Identify your logged-in username by executing the whoami command.

lessons.title: 2. Locate Directory (pwd)
lessons.definition: In Linux, your current location in the directory tree is the working directory.
lessons.explanation: The pwd command prints your absolute path from the root directory.
lessons.instruction: Check the current directory path in the terminal.

lessons.title: 3. List Files (ls)
lessons.definition: The ls command lists the files and directories inside your current folder.
lessons.explanation: By default, it shows visible files. You will see directories like drafts and welcome.txt.
lessons.instruction: List directory contents using the ls command.

lessons.title: 4. List Hidden Files (ls -a)
lessons.definition: Hidden files in Linux start with a dot (.) and contain configurations.
lessons.explanation: Passing -a to ls tells it to list all files, including hidden ones like .bashrc.
lessons.instruction: List all contents, including hidden configurations.

lessons.title: 5. List Details (ls -l)
lessons.definition: Long listing format displays permissions, owner, size, and modification date.
lessons.explanation: Using ls -l outputs detailed parameters of files in a table layout.
lessons.instruction: Run ls with the -l flag to view detailed file info.

lessons.title: 6. Navigate Into Folder (cd)
lessons.definition: The cd command changes your working directory location.
lessons.explanation: Specify the directory name to move inside. For example, cd drafts enters the drafts folder.
lessons.instruction: Change your active directory to drafts.

lessons.title: 7. Go Home (cd ~)
lessons.definition: The tilde symbol (~) represents your user's home directory path.
lessons.explanation: Running cd with ~ or no arguments instantly returns you to /home/student.
lessons.instruction: Navigate back to your home directory.

lessons.title: 8. Create File (touch)
lessons.definition: The touch command creates a new empty file.
lessons.explanation: If the file already exists, touch updates its last-modified timestamp.
lessons.instruction: Create a file named draft.txt in the current directory.

lessons.title: 9. Create Folder (mkdir)
lessons.definition: The mkdir command creates a new directory folder.
lessons.explanation: This allows you to organize files in subfolders. Use mkdir projects to create a folder.

lessons.title: 10. Write to File (echo >)
lessons.definition: The > operator redirects output to a file, overwriting its contents.
lessons.explanation: echo 'text' > file writes 'text' into the file. If the file doesn't exist, it is created.
lessons.instruction: Write the string 'Hello DevLab' into draft.txt.

lessons.title: 11. Append to File (echo >>)
lessons.definition: The >> operator appends content to the end of a file.
lessons.explanation: Unlike >, >> keeps the existing content of the file and adds new text below it.

lessons.title: 12. View File (cat)
lessons.definition: The cat command concatenates and displays the contents of a file.
lessons.explanation: It prints the entire file content directly to your terminal standard output.

lessons.title: 13. Copy File (cp)
lessons.definition: The cp command duplicates files or directories.
lessons.explanation: Specify the source file followed by the destination path.

lessons.title: 14. Move File (mv)
lessons.definition: The mv command moves or renames a file.
lessons.explanation: Moving relocates the file, removing it from the original path.
lessons.instruction: Move draft.txt into the projects directory.

lessons.title: 15. Delete File (rm)
lessons.definition: The rm command removes (deletes) files permanently.
lessons.explanation: Warning: files deleted with rm cannot be recovered in Linux!

exercises.title: Navigate and Explore
exercises.problem: Navigate to the root directory `/`, list its contents, then return home using `cd ~`.
exercises.hint: Use `cd /` to go to root, `ls` to list, then `cd ~` to return home.
exercises.objective: Practice absolute navigation and listing directory contents.

exercises.title: Build a Project Structure
exercises.problem: Create a folder called `myapp` with three subdirectories inside it: `src`, `config`, and `logs`.
exercises.hint: Use `mkdir myapp && mkdir myapp/src myapp/config myapp/logs` or `mkdir -p myapp/src myapp/config myapp/logs`.
exercises.expected_result: myapp directory with three subdirectories visible via ls myapp.

exercises.title: Write and Verify Config
exercises.problem: Create a file `myapp/config/app.env`, write `APP_ENV=production` into it, then verify the content with cat.
exercises.objective: Practice file creation, writing, and reading.

exercises.title: Backup Before Edit
exercises.problem: Backup `myapp/config/app.env` as `app.env.bak`, then append `LOG_LEVEL=debug` to the original file.
exercises.expected_result: cat app.env shows both APP_ENV=production and LOG_LEVEL=debug.

exercises.title: Save a Directory Listing
exercises.problem: List the contents of `/etc` and save the output into a file called `etc_contents.txt` in your home directory.
exercises.expected_result: etc_contents.txt in home directory containing the ls output.
exercises.objective: Practice stdout redirection to save command output to a file.

exercises.title: Find and Remove Temp Files
exercises.problem: Create three files: `temp1.log`, `temp2.log`, and `temp3.log`. Then delete all of them using a wildcard pattern.
exercises.hint: Create with `touch temp1.log temp2.log temp3.log`, delete with `rm temp*.log`.
exercises.expected_result: All three temp files are deleted. ls shows no temp*.log files.
exercises.objective: Practice wildcard patterns in file operations.

exercises.title: Reconstruct a Directory
exercises.problem: You accidentally deleted `myapp/logs`. Recreate it and add a file `app.log` with the content `App started`.
exercises.objective: Practice directory recreation and file writing under pressure.

exercises.title: Environment Variable Logging
exercises.problem: Write your system's `$USER`, `$HOME`, and `$PATH` environment variables into a file called `sysinfo.txt` (one per line, appended).
exercises.hint: Use three echo commands with >> : `echo $USER >> sysinfo.txt` then `echo $HOME >> sysinfo.txt` then `echo $PATH >> sysinfo.txt`.
exercises.expected_result: cat sysinfo.txt shows three lines with your username, home path, and PATH.
exercises.objective: Understand environment variables and file appending.

exercises.title: Deployment Script Scenario
exercises.problem: Simulate a deployment: create a `releases` folder, copy `myapp/config/app.env` into it as `v1.env`, then create a `releases/current` symlink placeholder file.
exercises.expected_result: ls -la releases shows v1.env and current.
exercises.objective: Simulate a real-world deployment directory structure setup.

exercises.title: Log Rotation Simulation
exercises.problem: Create a file `app.log` with 3 lines of content. Then rename it to `app.log.1`, create a new empty `app.log`, and append 'Log rotation complete' to it.
exercises.hint: Use `echo` three times with >>, then `mv app.log app.log.1`, `touch app.log`, and `echo 'Log rotation complete' >> app.log`.
exercises.expected_result: Two files: app.log.1 with original content, app.log with rotation message.
exercises.objective: Simulate a basic log rotation process used in production systems.

quiz.question: Which command shows your current directory path?
quiz.explanation: pwd (Print Working Directory) outputs the absolute path from root / to your current location.

incorrect_explanations.ls: ls lists files in a directory but does not show your current path.
incorrect_explanations.cd: cd changes directory but does not output your location.
incorrect_explanations.whoami: whoami shows your username, not your directory location.

quiz.question: Which flag shows hidden files (starting with a dot) when using ls?
quiz.explanation: The -a flag means 'all' — it includes hidden files that start with a dot like .bashrc.

incorrect_explanations.ls -l: Shows detailed metadata but does NOT show hidden files.
incorrect_explanations.ls -h: Makes file sizes human-readable (KB, MB) but does not reveal hidden files.
incorrect_explanations.ls -r: Reverses the sort order of the listing — does not show hidden files.

quiz.question: What does `cd` with no arguments do?
quiz.explanation: cd with no arguments defaults to the user's home directory, same as cd ~.

incorrect_explanations.Goes to root /: cd / goes to root. cd alone goes home.
incorrect_explanations.Shows an error: No arguments is valid syntax for cd.
incorrect_explanations.Shows help manual: Use cd --help to see the help page.

quiz.question: You run `echo 'DB_HOST=localhost' > config.env` on an existing file. What happens?
options: Text is appended to the end of the file
The file is deleted first, then recreated with the new text
The existing content is erased and replaced with DB_HOST=localhost
An error is thrown because the file already exists
quiz.answer: The existing content is erased and replaced with DB_HOST=localhost
quiz.explanation: The > operator truncates (clears) the file and writes new content. Use >> to append instead.

incorrect_explanations.The file is deleted first, then recreate: The file inode is reused, not deleted and recreated.
incorrect_explanations.An error is thrown because the file alre: > works whether the file exists or not.

quiz.question: Which command copies a file?
quiz.explanation: cp (copy) duplicates a file from a source path to a destination path.

incorrect_explanations.mv: mv moves or renames — the original is removed.
incorrect_explanations.touch: touch creates empty files or updates timestamps.

quiz.question: What does the `~` (tilde) symbol represent in Linux?
quiz.explanation: ~ is a shorthand for the current user's home directory, e.g., /home/student.

incorrect_explanations.The current directory: The current directory is represented by . (single dot).
incorrect_explanations.A hidden file marker: Hidden files start with a dot, not a tilde.

quiz.question: Which command permanently deletes a file in Linux?
quiz.explanation: rm (remove) permanently deletes files. There is no recycle bin in the Linux terminal.

incorrect_explanations.del: del is a Windows Command Prompt command, not Linux.
incorrect_explanations.trash: Not a standard Linux command — some desktop environments have trash-cli but it is not built-in.

quiz.question: You want to add a new line to an existing log file without erasing its contents. Which command do you use?
quiz.explanation: >> appends to the end of the file. The existing content is preserved.

incorrect_explanations.echo 'new entry' > app.log: > overwrites — existing log entries would be lost.
incorrect_explanations.cat 'new entry' > app.log: cat reads files, not strings. This would error.
incorrect_explanations.touch 'new entry' >> app.log: touch creates/updates timestamps — it does not write text.

quiz.question: What is the output of `pwd` if you are in /home/student/projects?
quiz.explanation: pwd always prints the full absolute path starting from /.

incorrect_explanations./home: This is only the home parent directory, not the full path.
incorrect_explanations./home/student: This is the user's home but you navigated into projects.
incorrect_explanations.~/projects: ~ is a shell shorthand — pwd always expands it to the full path.

quiz.question: You run `ls -la /etc` and see `-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1230 Jan 12 hosts`. What does the first `-rw-r--r--` mean?
options: A directory readable by root only
A file that root can read and write, others can only read
A file that everyone can read and write
An executable file owned by root
quiz.answer: A file that root can read and write, others can only read
quiz.explanation: -rw-r--r-- means: regular file (-), owner (root) has read+write (rw-), group has read-only (r--), others have read-only (r--).

incorrect_explanations.A directory readable by root only: Directories start with d. This starts with - meaning regular file.
incorrect_explanations.A file that everyone can read and write: Only the owner (root) has write permission. Others only have read.
incorrect_explanations.An executable file owned by root: Executable files show x in permissions. This shows rw- with no x.

quiz.question: Which command creates a directory called `projects`?
quiz.explanation: mkdir (make directory) creates a new folder. touch creates files, not directories.

incorrect_explanations.touch projects: touch creates files. It would create a file named projects, not a folder.
incorrect_explanations.cd projects: cd changes directory — it cannot create one.

quiz.question: Scenario: You accidentally deleted an important file. Can you recover it using a Linux terminal command?
options: No — rm permanently deletes files with no built-in recovery
quiz.answer: No — rm permanently deletes files with no built-in recovery
quiz.explanation: Linux has no built-in file recovery for rm. The file blocks are marked as free immediately. Always have backups.

incorrect_explanations.Yes, use `restore filename`: restore is not a standard command for recovering rm-deleted files.
incorrect_explanations.Yes, use `undelete filename`: undelete exists in some legacy tools but not in standard Linux CLI.
incorrect_explanations.Yes, use `rm -u filename`: -u is not a valid flag for rm.

resources.summary: This module covers the Linux CLI fundamentals: navigation (pwd, cd), file listing (ls), file operations (touch, mkdir, echo, cat, cp, mv, rm), and redirection operators (>, >>). These are the daily tools of every DevOps engineer.

links.description: Interactive beginner-friendly Linux learning with hands-on exercises.

resources.cheat_sheet:
# Linux Command Line Basics Cheat Sheet

## Navigation
pwd                    # Show current directory
cd [folder]            # Go to folder
cd ~                   # Go to home
cd ..                  # Go up one level
cd -                   # Go to previous directory

## Listing Files
ls                     # List visible files
ls -a                  # Show hidden files
ls -l                  # Long format (permissions, owner, size)
ls -la                 # Long format + hidden files
ls -lah                # Long format + hidden + human-readable sizes

## File Operations
touch file             # Create empty file
mkdir folder           # Create directory
mkdir -p a/b/c         # Create nested directories
echo 'text' > file     # Write text to file (overwrites)
echo 'text' >> file    # Append text to file
cat file               # Display file contents
cp src dest            # Copy file
mv src dest            # Move or rename file
rm file                # Delete file
rm -rf folder          # Delete directory recursively

## Shortcuts
~                      # Home directory
.                      # Current directory
..                     # Parent directory
Tab                    # Auto-complete filenames

commands_table.name: cd
commands_table.description: Change to specified directory. No args = home.

commands_table.name: ls -la
commands_table.description: List all files with full details including hidden.

commands_table.name: touch
commands_table.description: Create empty file or update timestamp.

commands_table.name: echo
commands_table.description: Write or append text to a file.

revision_notes: The root directory / is the parent of ALL files in Linux.
Always run pwd before rm to confirm your location.
Use ls -la to reveal hidden config files starting with a dot.
The > operator overwrites. The >> operator appends.
rm is permanent — always backup before deleting important files.
best_practices: Never run commands as root unless you specifically need elevated privileges.
Always backup config files before modifying: cp file.conf file.conf.bak
Use tab completion to avoid typos in filenames.
Create organized directory structures: separate src, config, logs, and docs folders.
beginner_mistakes: Typing `cd..` without a space — correct is `cd ..`.
Using > instead of >> and overwriting important log files.
Running rm without checking pwd first — files can be deleted from the wrong directory.
Using backslashes \ for paths — Linux only uses forward slashes /.

interview_questions.question: What is the difference between an absolute path and a relative path?
interview_questions.answer: Absolute paths start from root / (e.g., /home/student/projects). Relative paths start from your current location (e.g., projects or ../other).

interview_questions.question: How do you append a line to a file without deleting existing content?
interview_questions.answer: Use echo 'text' >> filename. The >> operator appends to the end. Single > overwrites.

interview_questions.question: What does ls -la tell you that ls alone does not?
interview_questions.answer: ls -la shows hidden files (starting with .), file permissions (rwx), owner, group, file size, and modification date.

books.title: The Linux Command Line — William Shotts
books.description: The definitive beginner guide. Available free at linuxcommand.org.

books.title: Linux Pocket Guide — Daniel J. Barrett
books.description: Compact quick reference for the most common Linux commands.
